Sub-questions: Is this recovery safe enough to start? Are there any immediate no-go conditions? Is the scene controlled enough to proceed? Is there any reason to hold or abort before rigging begins?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT diagnose why the casualty is stuck, does NOT inventory equipment, and does NOT choose or build the rig.
Exit criteria:
☐ Immediate no-go hazards have been identified and either controlled or declared unacceptable
☐ Scene is controlled enough to proceed
☐ Operator has made a formal Go / Hold / Abort decision
☐ Continue only on Go
Technical debt: Design glovebox-simplified ICS form alternatives + form disclaimer

Main question: Why is the casualty not moving?
What that section owns: Owns diagnosis of the physical problem
Sub-questions: Is it traction loss, suction, belly drag, obstacle interference, slope, side-load, or mechanical bind? What resistance is preventing movement? What physical condition of vehicle, terrain, and load is causing the problem?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT decide whether to engage, does NOT assign roles, and does NOT choose the rig solution.
Exit criteria:
☐ Casualty problem has been defined clearly enough to describe the recovery problem in one plain statement
☐ Main resisting mechanisms are identified
☐ Terrain, slope/deviation, and physical extraction constraints are observed
☐ Situation is defined well enough to move into capability matching
Exit criteria artifact: ICS 201 Section 3 Map/Sketch + Section 5 Situation Summary / Health and Safety Briefing completed

Main question: What capability do I have to rig with?
Sub-questions: What vehicles, winches, anchors, lines, hardware, tools, and PPE do I have? What is serviceable? What is rated? What is missing? What limits do these components impose?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT explain why the casualty is stuck, does NOT justify the rig concept, and does NOT assign the execution plan.
Exit criteria:
☐ Required equipment and support items have been identified
☐ Relevant gear is present, compatible, serviceable, and appropriately rated
☐ Critical deficiencies are either resolved or recognized as stopping conditions
☐ There is enough capability for at least one valid rig option

Main question: Why is this rig the solution?
Sub-questions: Why direct pull, double line, redirect, bridle, anchor array, or another layout? Why this extraction direction? Why this line path? Why this level of mechanical advantage? Why is this concept better than the alternatives?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT inventory gear, does NOT assign exact people/positions/timing, and does NOT teach the physical setup steps.
Exit criteria:
☐ One rig concept has been selected and justified against the baseline problem and available capability
☐ Extraction direction, anchor concept, line path, and broad rig architecture are chosen
☐ Concept is clear enough to sketch and confirm before detailed planning

Main question: What exactly is this rig plan?
What that section owns: Owns decision architecture: limits, math, and sequence
Sub-questions: Does this configuration stay within the Working Load Limits of the gear chosen? What is the Mechanical Advantage? What exact rig configuration will be used? Who does what? Where does each element go? What is the exact sequence of events for staging, testing, and pulling?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT debate why this concept was chosen and does NOT teach the physical handwork of connecting or routing the rig.
Exit criteria:
☐ Chosen concept has been converted into an exact execution plan
☐ Math and WLL are confirmed safe
☐ Roles are assigned
☐ Sequence, objectives, hold points, and stop triggers are defined
☐ Plan is explicit enough to brief without improvisation
Exit criteria artifact: ICS 201 Section 7 Current and Planned Objectives + Section 9 Current Organization completed + Readiness Buddy-Checklist completed

Sub-questions: How do I physically connect it? How do I route the line? How do I wrap the anchor, position the blocks, and secure the dampers?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT re-justify the rig concept, does NOT dictate the timing/sequence of the pull, and does NOT diagnose the casualty.
Exit criteria:
☐ Rig has been physically built to plan
☐ All connections, routing, protection, dampers, and control measures are in place
☐ Communications/signals are confirmed
☐ Pre-pull physical checks are passed
☐ System is declared ready to pull

Main question: Execute, then what is happening now?
Sub-questions: Is the plan working? Is the vehicle moving as expected? Is the line tracking correctly? Is anything worsening, shifting, binding, or becoming unsafe? Do I continue, pause, re-rig, or abort?
Hard exclusion rule: Does NOT create the original plan, does NOT inventory equipment, and does NOT teach setup from scratch; it governs live action and sends the operator back through the loop when reality changes.
Exit criteria:
☐ Pull has been executed and monitored
☐ Live outcome has been assessed
☐ Decision has been made to continue, pause, re-rig, abort, or complete
☐ Any material change has been recorded and, if needed, sent back into the earlier sections for revision
Exit criteria artifact: ICS 214 Activity Log completed + ICS 201 updated
Technical debt: Add EXTENSION tear-down pages (Winch cool-down, hot-spooling prevention, wet rope storage, post-pull inspection)
